personality_voice.book_baseline: Crouch Sr. in the books is defined by rigid formality, cold authority, and an obsessive commitment to rules and reputation. His speech is curt, impatient, and clipped — he cuts Bagman off mid-sentence, dismisses Percy without learning his name ('Weatherby'), and delivers pronouncements rather than conversations. He is capable of explosive rage (shouting at Diggory, bellowing at his son in the Pensieve trial), but his default register is icy control. He is deeply contradictory: publicly the most law-abiding Ministry official, privately capable of sending his own son to Azkaban and later smuggling him out. His deterioration — from crisp authority at the World Cup to the babbling, disheveled wreck in the forest — is a carefully tracked psychological collapse.
personality_voice.film_portrayal: The film preserves the surface markers: the stiff bearing, the curt delivery, the formal diction. His announcement of the age restriction rule ('After due consideration, the Ministry has concluded...') and his handling of the champions' draw ('The rules are absolute. The Goblet of Fire constitutes a binding magical contract') are consistent with his book voice. The Pensieve scene captures his cold prosecutorial manner and his disowning of his son ('You are no son of mine'). However, the film omits the full arc of his personality deterioration — his increasingly ill appearance across multiple scenes, his delusional forest breakdown, and the contrast between his earlier rigidity and his later collapse are almost entirely absent.
personality_voice.difference: The film shows Crouch at two points: functional authority figure and Pensieve flashback. The middle stages — his suspicious absences, his visibly worsening health, his midnight break-in to Snape's office, and above all his deranged appearance in the forest (talking to trees, mistaking Harry for Percy, clutching at robes) — are cut entirely. The film never shows the psychological disintegration that makes his book personality so distinctive. His explosive rage at Winky and Diggory after the Dark Mark is also absent from the film.
personality_voice.penalty_logic: The core personality markers (cold formality, rule-obsession, curt speech) are preserved in the scenes that exist. However, the loss of his deterioration arc and his unhinged forest appearance removes a defining dimension of his personality — the crack beneath the rigid surface. The Winky dismissal scene, which shows his cruelty and his inability to show pity, is absent. These are not peripheral traits; they are what make him more than a bureaucratic functionary. Score reflects recognizable but significantly incomplete portrayal.

narrative_role_agency.book_baseline: Crouch Sr. serves multiple narrative functions: (1) he is the authoritative Ministry voice who sets the tournament rules; (2) he is a mystery figure whose suspicious absences drive a subplot; (3) his forest appearance is a pivotal plot event that Harry actively investigates and reports to Dumbledore; (4) his history as a ruthless Dark wizard hunter contextualizes the moral ambiguity of the wizarding world's response to Voldemort; (5) his death (transformed into a bone) is a concrete plot point confirming Voldemort's return and Barty Jr.'s actions. He initiates the mystery subplot, drives Harry's curiosity, and his confession (via his son) is the mechanism by which the full truth is revealed.
narrative_role_agency.film_portrayal: In the film, Crouch Sr. is reduced to two functional appearances: announcing the age restriction rule at the feast, and presiding over the champions' draw. He appears briefly in the Pensieve (Karkaroff's trial and his son's trial). He is seen dead on the ground near Hagrid's hut in a single shot, with no dialogue or investigation. His role as a mystery figure — the suspicious absences, the midnight office break-in, the forest encounter — is entirely cut.
narrative_role_agency.difference: The entire mystery subplot surrounding Crouch Sr. is eliminated. Harry never investigates him, never sees him on the Marauder's Map, never races to fetch Dumbledore. The forest scene — one of the most dramatically significant appearances of any secondary character in GoF — does not exist in the film. His death is a background visual rather than a plot-driving revelation. His narrative function is reduced to 'Ministry official who announces rules.'
narrative_role_agency.penalty_logic: This is a substantial agency loss. In the books, Crouch Sr. is an active driver of the mystery plot — his behavior causes Harry to investigate, his forest appearance is a crisis Harry must respond to, and his death is a concrete clue. In the film, he is passive and decorative in all but the Pensieve scenes. The transfer rule applies: the mystery and investigation around Crouch Sr. simply does not exist in the film, rather than being transferred to another character.

motivations_internal_conflict.book_baseline: Crouch Sr.'s motivations are layered: a genuine (if extreme) commitment to defeating dark wizards; an overriding obsession with reputation and career advancement; a cold inability to prioritize family over ambition (sending his son to Azkaban, never visiting); and a late-stage guilt and desperation to confess and warn Dumbledore, suggesting some residual conscience. His internal conflict — between his public image as the law's champion and his private actions (smuggling his son out of Azkaban, covering it up) — is the engine of his arc.
motivations_internal_conflict.film_portrayal: The film establishes his rule-obsession and his willingness to send his son to Azkaban ('You are no son of mine' in the Pensieve). Sirius's line in the fireplace scene — 'Heart of stone. Sent his own son to Azkaban' — provides a brief external characterization. However, the guilt, the late-stage desperation to confess, and the internal conflict between his public and private selves are not dramatized. The film does not show him under the Imperius Curse, does not show his attempted confession, and does not show the complexity of his relationship with his son's imprisonment.
motivations_internal_conflict.difference: The motivational complexity is present in outline (rule-obsession, ruthlessness) but the internal conflict — the guilt, the cover-up, the desperate attempt to warn Dumbledore — is absent because the scenes that dramatize it are cut. The film viewer understands he is harsh and rule-bound but not that he is a man destroyed by the consequences of his own choices.
motivations_internal_conflict.penalty_logic: Motivations are partially present but significantly flattened. The film gives enough to understand his public persona but not the private conflict that makes him a genuinely complex figure. The score reflects that the core motivation (rules above all, reputation above family) is present, but the contradictory layer (guilt, attempted redemption) is lost.
motivations_internal_conflict.evidence_status: Reasonably inferred from what the film includes; directly evidenced for what is absent

character_arc.book_baseline: Crouch Sr.'s arc in GoF runs from powerful-but-declining Ministry official → increasingly absent and mysterious figure → deranged, Imperius-controlled puppet → desperate escapee trying to warn Dumbledore → murdered by his own son. It is a tragedy of a man whose ruthlessness destroyed his family and ultimately himself. The arc has a clear starting point (World Cup: still authoritative, if cold), a middle (deterioration, absences, the mystery), and an end (forest breakdown, death, posthumous revelation via his son's confession).
character_arc.film_portrayal: The film shows the starting point (authoritative Ministry official at the feast and champions' draw) and the endpoint (dead on the ground, Barty Jr.'s confession). The entire middle — the deterioration, the absences, the mystery, the forest breakdown — is cut. The arc exists only as a before-and-after with no dramatized journey between them.
character_arc.difference: The arc is structurally present in the film only as a skeleton: he appears, then he is dead, then his son confesses. The steps that constitute the arc — the progressive loss of control, the desperate attempt to break free and warn Dumbledore — are absent. The audience has no experience of his decline.
character_arc.penalty_logic: The arc is incomplete in a way that changes the character. The film's Crouch Sr. does not decline — he simply disappears and is then reported dead. The emotional and dramatic weight of his arc (a man destroyed by his own choices, making one last attempt at conscience) is lost. This is not compression that preserves the arc; it is omission that eliminates it.

key_relationships.book_baseline: Three relationships define Crouch Sr.: (1) with Barty Jr. — a relationship of cold rejection, public disowning, secret rescue, and ultimate murder; (2) with Winky — a relationship that reveals his cruelty, his obsession with reputation, and his inability to show compassion to those beneath him; (3) with Percy — a comic-ironic relationship in which Percy's hero-worship of Crouch exposes Crouch's obliviousness and social coldness (he never learns Percy's name). Each relationship reveals a different facet of his character.
key_relationships.film_portrayal: The father-son relationship is present in the Pensieve ('You are no son of mine') and in Barty Jr.'s confession. The Winky relationship is entirely absent from the film — Winky does not appear. The Percy relationship is absent. The emotional dynamic of the father-son relationship is present in outline but lacks the full context: the secret rescue, the Imperius Curse, the years of imprisonment at home, the wife's sacrifice — all of which deepen the relationship's tragedy.
key_relationships.difference: The Winky relationship — which is the primary vehicle for showing Crouch's cruelty and his treatment of inferiors — is entirely absent. The Percy relationship, which provides ironic texture, is absent. The father-son relationship is present but compressed to the point of losing the contradictions (he sent him to Azkaban AND secretly rescued him) that make it dramatically rich.
key_relationships.penalty_logic: Two of three key relationships are absent entirely. The remaining relationship (father-son) is present but significantly simplified. This substantially changes how the character is understood — without Winky, his cruelty to inferiors is not dramatized; without Percy, his social obliviousness is not shown. Cap 2 (above 80 if key relationships change substantially) is relevant here.

complexity_nuance_lost_material.book_baseline: Crouch Sr. is one of the most morally complex secondary characters in GoF. He is simultaneously: a genuine hero of the fight against Voldemort; a man who became as ruthless as the Dark wizards he fought; a cold father who sacrificed his son for his career; a man who then secretly rescued that son out of love for his wife; a man kept prisoner in his own home by his Imperius-controlled self; and finally a man trying to do the right thing at the end. The Sirius exposition scene, the Pensieve trials, the Winky subplot, and the forest scene together build a portrait of extraordinary complexity.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.film_portrayal: The film retains: his cold public authority, his rule-obsession, his disowning of his son (Pensieve), and his death. It loses: the Winky subplot and dismissal (cruelty to inferiors, reputation obsession), the mystery subplot (suspicious absences, Marauder's Map, Snape's office), the forest breakdown (psychological collapse, guilt, attempted confession), the Sirius backstory scene (his history as a ruthless Dark wizard hunter, his fall from grace), and the full context of the father-son relationship (the rescue, the Imperius imprisonment, the wife's sacrifice).
complexity_nuance_lost_material.difference: The film's Crouch Sr. is a one-dimensional bureaucratic authority figure with a tragic backstory glimpsed in the Pensieve. The book's Crouch Sr. is a man whose entire life story — ambition, ruthlessness, family tragedy, guilt, and attempted redemption — is dramatized across the novel. The complexity that makes him memorable is almost entirely absent from the film.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.penalty_logic: This is major flattening. The character is reduced to his function (Ministry official, rule-enforcer) and a single dramatic moment (disowning his son). The contradictions, the moral ambiguity, the psychological collapse, and the relational texture are gone. Cap 1 (above 85 if major complexity is removed) and Cap 3 (above 75 if core agency/decisions are transferred or removed) are both relevant.

lost_or_transferred_material: Entire mystery subplot: Crouch Sr.'s suspicious absences from the tournament and World Cup Top Box
Crouch Sr. seen on the Marauder's Map breaking into Snape's office at night
Harry's investigation of Crouch Sr. and reporting to Sirius
Sirius's extended backstory about Crouch Sr. — his rise, his ruthless methods, his authorized use of Unforgivable Curses, his fall from grace
Crouch Sr.'s forest breakdown scene: deranged, disheveled, talking to trees, mistaking Harry for Percy, begging to warn Dumbledore
Crouch Sr.'s attempted confession and warning to Dumbledore
The Winky subplot entirely: Winky saving Crouch a seat, the Dark Mark confrontation, Crouch dismissing Winky, Winky's grief at Hogwarts
Crouch Sr.'s cruelty to Winky — 'There is no pity in his gaze' — as a character-defining moment
Crouch Sr.'s explosive rage at Diggory and the Ministry wizards after the Dark Mark
Percy's hero-worship of Crouch and Crouch never learning Percy's name ('Weatherby') — ironic texture
Crouch Sr.'s visibly deteriorating health across multiple scenes
The full context of the father-son relationship: the secret rescue from Azkaban, the wife's sacrifice, the years of Imperius imprisonment at home
Moody's line contextualizing Crouch: 'they say old Mad-Eye's obsessed with catching Dark wizards... but I'm nothing compared to Barty Crouch'
Crouch Sr.'s death being dramatized as a plot point rather than shown as a background visual
score_caps_applied: Cap 1 (no score above 85): major complexity, agency, contradiction, and narrative function removed — applied
Cap 2 (no score above 80): key relationships (Winky, Percy) substantially changed or absent — applied
Cap 3 (no score above 75): core agency (mystery subplot, forest scene, attempted confession) removed rather than transferred — applied
comparative.key_observations: The film preserves Crouch Sr.'s surface identity — cold, formal, rule-obsessed, capable of disowning his son — but removes almost everything that makes him a complex character in the books. The entire mystery subplot he drives (suspicious absences, Marauder's Map, forest breakdown, attempted confession) is cut, eliminating his narrative agency. The Winky relationship, which is the primary vehicle for his cruelty and reputation-obsession, is absent entirely. The Sirius backstory that contextualizes his moral ambiguity is reduced to a single throwaway line. What remains is a functional bureaucrat with a tragic Pensieve cameo — recognizable but hollowed out. The film's Crouch Sr. is a sketch of the book's Crouch Sr.
